metadata.summary: Map per-item LLM work with Send/ParallelAgent, cap at 20 concurrency, require idempotency — super-steps fail atomically so non-idempotent work double-applies on retry.

content.title: Map-Reduce Fan-Out with Bounded Concurrency and Idempotent Branches
content.summary: Use dynamic fan-out (Send(node, payload) in LangGraph, ParallelAgent in ADK, asyncio.gather in plain Python) to map a per-item LLM call across a list, then reduce with an annotated reducer (Annotated[list, operator.add] or a custom merge). Cap concurrency at ≤20 to avoid rate-limit cliffs, and design every branch to be idempotent + retryable because any single branch failure fails the entire super-step atomically. Never use map-reduce fan-out for branches that mutate shared external state — use a queue + workers instead.

why.p: Latency in agent stacks is dominated by serialized LLM calls. For embarrassingly parallel work (per-document scoring, multi-source RAG, multi-candidate generation, batch tool calls over a list), map-reduce drops wall time from O(N · turn) to O(turn) — a 5-20× latency win on N=20 — and lets you route each branch to a cheaper model (Haiku per item, Sonnet for the reduce). The constraints exist because rate-limit cliffs are real (most providers rate-limit hard at 60-300 req/s, and parallel branches share that budget) and because LangGraph's super-step semantics fail atomically — a non-idempotent branch on the failing path produces a half-applied side effect on retry.

list.item: Per-document scoring or classification across N documents.
Multi-source RAG fan-out (vector search + web search + memory lookup, then merge).
Multi-candidate generation (sample N drafts, pick best with a reducer / vote).
Batch tool calls over a list (enrich N rows, classify N emails) where each item is independent.
Fixed-N parallel where N is known at graph-compile time — use static fan-out edges; Send is for runtime-variable N.
Branches that mutate shared external state (write to the same DB row, append to one file) — use a queue + workers, not Send. Atomic-fail of the super-step double-applies on retry.
N > 20 with a single provider — split into batches, otherwise rate-limit kills the run; or move to provider Batch API.
Strong consistency requirements across branches (every branch must see the same prior state) — use sequential.

section.name: bounded-fan-out-rule
section.p: Dynamic fan-out turns serial agent calls into parallel ones, but only if branches are independent and retryable. The shape is: a router node emits N Send commands, each carrying a per-item payload; an aggregator node reads the reduced list.

rule.statement: Use map-reduce fan-out (Send / ParallelAgent / asyncio.gather) only when (a) per-item work is independent, (b) every branch is idempotent and retryable, (c) the target state field has a reducer annotation, and (d) concurrency is capped at ≤20.
rule.rationale: LangGraph super-steps fail atomically — one branch crashing reverts the whole step's writes. Without idempotency, retry double-applies side effects. Without a reducer, parallel writes to the same key clobber each other. Above 20 concurrent calls, most providers' rate limits kick in and tail latency dominates the parallelism win.

example.note: Reducer annotation, cap of 20, pure scoring branch. Reads safely on retry.
200 concurrent Sends saturate the rate limit; non-idempotent DB write double-applies on super-step retry.

antipattern.description: Forgetting the reducer annotation on the aggregation field.
antipattern.reason: Parallel branches each overwrite the field, last write wins, all but one branch's result is lost. The Annotated[list, operator.add] (or a custom merge) is mandatory.

antipattern.description: Using Send for branches that mutate shared external state (S3 manifest, DB row, append-only file).
antipattern.reason: Super-step atomic failure means partial side effects survive a retry. Use a queue + workers (SQS, Inngest, Temporal) for write fan-out, never Send.

list.item: Default cap: 20 concurrent branches per super-step. Above that, most provider rate limits dominate latency.
Anthropic Batch API / OpenAI Batch — use for N > 100 with no real-time deadline; 50% cheaper, no rate-limit risk.
For N in 20-100 with deadline: chunk into batches of 20, run sequentially, accumulate.
Mix model tiers per branch: cheap model for the map, strong for the reduce — extra parallel tax stays in the cheap budget.

template.purpose: LangGraph reference: router emits Send(...) per item, reducer aggregates with operator.add.

see-also.link: LangGraph — Use the Graph API (Send semantics).
Map-Reduce with Send() in LangGraph — concrete walkthrough.
Scaling LangGraph Agents — parallelization, subgraphs, map-reduce.
